research_answer: The Bangladesh garment industry offers a striking case study in how export industries can emerge from a single foreign catalyst. One Korean company entered Bangladesh and trained 126 workers. Those workers subsequently went on to start 56 new high-performing companies. This spinout dynamic — where knowledge and capabilities embodied in workers spill over into new firms — effectively seeded an entire export sector from a single point of entry. The implication for trade and diversification is significant: gaining a foothold in a new export product can have compounding effects far beyond the initial firm, as tacit productive knowledge diffuses through the workforce into new enterprises. This mechanism helps explain why export diversification tends to be path-dependent and clustered — new industries don't emerge randomly, but grow from existing nodes of capability.
supporting_quotes: Mostafa and Klepper (2018) show how the garment industry was seeded into Bangladesh by one Korean company that trained 126 workers who went on to start 56 new high-performing companies.

data_points.metric: industry seeding: workers trained and firms created
